course_title: Transnational Crime and International Criminal Justice

learning_outcomes.description: Have a general understanding of the patterns and levels of transnational organised crime and terrorism.
Describe and critique the nature and problems of transnational organised crime and terrorism.

Identify key instruments and the strengths and weaknesses of the current system of international criminal justice, especially as it relates to the criminalisation of transnational crime and terrorism and enforcement.
Navigate the complexity of international and transnational legal, policy, and institutional contexts in order to be able to effectively engage with key debates emerging in this area.

Work together with others to identify the dimensions of a problem arising from a case study of global or transnational crime, to generate solutions to the problem, and to communicate this to an informed audience.
Apply skills required to engage in effective legal and academic research, and to communicate effectively within a legal and policy context.

assessments.task_description:
The first exam covers topics 1 and 2 of the course (weeks 3 to 5 of the semester). It will be undertaken in person, on-campus, and online. The exam consists of 20 randomly assigned multiple choice questions (50 marks) and one short essay question (50 marks). Students have 35 minutes to complete the exam.

Exams 1 and 2 are invigilated and constitute the secure assessment component in this course. They will take place in-class on Blackboard using the students' own laptop (or similar device).

Further instructions along with a sample test will be provided on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia
assessments.task_description:
Students participate in a group project to work on a designated research topic and present in a video recording which will be posted online. Ideally, each groups involves a minimum of 3 and no more than 5 persons).

Additional information about the projects and modes of presentations (PowerPoint, handouts) will be supplied at the beginning of the course on Blackboard in the 'Assessment' folder.  The topics will be discussed and groups will be created in the first class (week 3).  Groups will mainly work on their project in the class-free period (weeks 6, 7, 8, and 9), record their presentation in a 15mins (max) video, and submit the video file before the due date.

The video presentation (including handouts, visualisation, clarity, consistency, discussion etc) is the element of assessment and the grade will apply to all members of the working group. 

Further instructions for this assessment item will be released and discussed in August.

Where a student's SAP requires this, this task may undertaken by students working individually. This will require prior approval by the course-coordinator.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students submit their video presentation as a single file via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The second exam covers topics 3 and 4 of the course (weeks 10 and 11 of the semester). It will be undertaken in person, on-campus, and online. The exam consists of 15 randomly assigned multiple choice questions (50 marks) and one short essay question (50 marks). Students have 25 minutes to complete the exam.

Exams 1 and 2 are invigilated and constitute the secure assessment component in this course. They will take place in-class on Blackboard using the students' own laptop (or similar device).

Further instructions along with a sample test will be provided on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 3 To Week 12(10 Aug - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

This course will cover the following topics:

  • Week 3 - Introduction to the course; Organised Crime (part 1)
  • Week 4 - Organised Crime (part 2)
  • Week 5 - Drug Trafficking
  • Week 10 - Smuggling of migrants & trafficking in persons (part 1)
  • Week 11 - Smuggling of migrants & trafficking in persons (part 2)
  • Week 12 - Money laundering & corruption

(No seminars weeks 1 - 2 or 6 - 9)


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
